honestyNote: This library encodes CRAFT PATTERNS with citations. There is no public database of 'resumes selected at company X' and this library does not pretend one exists. Per-company insight comes from Intel/JD analysis at tailor time. Every pattern below carries the source(s) it was derived from and the date it was verified. Stale entries (>12 months) must be flagged by Pulse, never silently trusted.

patterns.rule: Prefer bullets shaped 'Accomplished [X] as measured by [Y] by doing [Z]' — outcome first, measurement explicit, method last.
patterns.why: Google's own recruiters (via Laszlo Bock) teach this shape because it forces metrics and concrete results into every line; it reads as impact, not duty.
patterns.exemplar: Structure: <result verb> <thing> <measured by N> <by doing method>. e.g. 'Cut export failures to zero (100% parse-back fidelity) by drawing PDF text line-by-line with a deterministic renderer.'

patterns.rule: Optimize the top third of the page: name, headline, strongest project titles and their first bullets must carry the argument alone.
patterns.why: Eye-tracking shows recruiters average 7.4 seconds on the first skim, reading in an E/F pattern — top-left content and bold titles get almost all fixation time.
patterns.exemplar: The #1-cast project sits first; its first bullet is its strongest measured outcome, not its setup.

patterns.rule: Single column, standard section headings, no tables, no text boxes, no header/footer content, 'Month YYYY' dates.
patterns.why: Parser tests across 8 ATS systems show tables and multi-column layouts scramble or drop content; single-column plain text parses reliably everywhere (Greenhouse included).
patterns.exemplar: Already structural in SIFARISH (D5): the compiler emits a plain single-column document; this pattern is the estimator's parse-structure check.

patterns.rule: Every bullet starts with a strong past-tense action verb; never 'Responsible for', never a noun phrase, never the same verb twice in one entry.
patterns.why: Harvard and Stanford career guides both teach verb-first bullets; repeated or weak verbs read as duty lists and fail the skim.
patterns.exemplar: Strong: Built, Shipped, Cut, Reduced, Designed, Engineered, Automated, Benchmarked, Instrumented. Weak: Worked on, Helped, Was responsible for, Participated in.

patterns.rule: Surface every real number the evidence holds (users, latency, size, count, %, time saved). Never invent one — a bullet with no evidenced number stays number-free.
patterns.why: Recruiter surveys report quantified achievements as a top shortlisting factor; numbers give the skim something to fixate on. Honesty is structural in SIFARISH (I1): metrics come from the ledger only.
patterns.exemplar: Ledger has '29 ATS boards probed live' → the bullet says '29 live-probed ATS boards', not 'multiple job boards'.

patterns.rule: For a candidate without formal work history, Projects IS the experience section: 3-4 deep projects with stack, 2-3 outcome bullets each, and a live link — placed above Education.
patterns.why: Intern-focused guides (Tech Interview Handbook, Interview Kickstart, freeCodeCamp) converge: shipped personal projects with metrics fill the experience gap and are weighted heavily for interns.
patterns.exemplar: PROJECT TITLE — one-line what-it-is · stack · live URL, then bullets. Depth beats count: 3 projects with outcomes > 6 titles.

patterns.rule: Skills section: 8-15 skills grouped by category (Languages / AI-LLM / Frameworks / Tools), spelled exactly as the JD spells them, and only skills the candidate can defend in an interview.
patterns.why: ATS keyword match is exact-string in many systems ('JavaScript' ≠ 'JS'); grouped short lists scan better than laundry lists, and unlisted-but-undefendable skills fail interviews.
patterns.exemplar: Languages: Python, TypeScript · AI/LLM: RAG, agent orchestration, evals · Tools: Git, Docker.

patterns.rule: Zero invisible text, zero keyword stuffing, zero pasted-JD tricks. Keywords appear only where evidence exists.
patterns.why: ATS platforms and recruiters detect white-fonting (Ctrl-A, parser diffs); it converts a maybe into a blacklist. Structural in SIFARISH: keywords without ledger evidence are a hard gate failure.
patterns.exemplar: JD coverage gate: matched keywords all carry ledgerIds; unevidenced keywords appearing anywhere = 0 tolerance.

patterns.rule: Exactly one page for <2 years experience. Education carries CGPA (if ≥7.0), institute, and dates. No photo, no DOB, no marital status, no declaration for private-sector tech.
patterns.why: Indian tech recruiters treat two pages from a fresher as poor prioritization; CGPA is expected for 0-3 year candidates; personal-detail blocks are outdated and invite bias.
patterns.exemplar: Structural in SIFARISH (D32): the compiler solves one-page by progressive trim.

patterns.rule: Past tense for completed work, present tense only for ongoing items (the 'Currently Building' line). Never mix tenses inside one entry; keep bullets grammatically parallel.
patterns.why: Career guides converge on tense consistency as a readability and parser signal; irregular structure disrupts both the human skim and keyword extraction.
patterns.exemplar: Shipped project → 'Built…', 'Cut…'. In-forge line → 'Building…' with its honest date (I2).

patterns.rule: The headline under the name mirrors the target role family in plain words (no title inflation), e.g. 'AI engineering intern candidate — shipped LLM/agent systems'.
patterns.why: The skim's first fixation is name + headline; a headline aligned to the opening tells the reviewer they're in the right pile within the first second.
patterns.exemplar: Headline states what he builds and the strongest true credential, not adjectives.

patterns.rule: For applied-AI/agent/platform roles, lead with deployment, evals, guardrails, latency/cost — end-to-end ownership language — not model theory.
patterns.why: AI-hiring reviewers reject notebook-only profiles; production signals (serving, monitoring, evals, budgets) are the differentiator recruiters search for.
patterns.exemplar: Bullet foregrounds 'deployed', 'evaluated against N-case suite', 'p95 latency', 'keyless fallback', 'budget-capped' over 'trained a model'.

patterns.rule: For research roles, lead with method + measured evaluation + honesty about limits; include math/foundations and any reproducible artifacts (repo, report).
patterns.why: Research labs and residencies explicitly screen for rigorous evaluation, open-source artifacts, and depth over breadth.
patterns.exemplar: Bullet shape: method → experiment → measured result → what didn't work (honest limitation reads as rigor).

patterns.rule: Cover letter: 250-450 words, 3-4 paragraphs. Open with the strongest role-relevant proof, name something specific about the company (cited), close with one plain ask.
patterns.why: Hiring managers skim letters; specificity to the company is the credibility test, and the strongest qualification must land in the first two sentences.
patterns.exemplar: Para 1: strongest proof + why this company (cited intel). Para 2: one project story with numbers. Para 3: the dated-momentum line + ask.

patterns.rule: Every shipped project carries a working URL (live app or repo). A dead link is worse than no link.
patterns.why: For portfolio-driven AI hiring, reviewers click; a live artifact is the single strongest honesty signal a student resume can carry. Probe-gated in SIFARISH.
patterns.exemplar: Title line: NAME — what it is · live URL. Baithak attaches links only after a liveness probe.

patterns.rule: For remote-international roles, surface timezone (IST, UTC+5:30), written-communication artifacts (docs, READMEs), and any async-collaboration evidence.
patterns.why: Distributed teams screen for async writing ability and timezone honesty; guides for Indian candidates applying internationally converge on stating both explicitly.
patterns.exemplar: Location line: 'Lucknow, India (IST, UTC+5:30) · open to async-first remote'. Bullets cite documentation/README quality where evidenced.

patterns.rule: Cut objective statements, references-available, soft-skill adjective lists, and any course/tool with no evidence behind it. White space is a feature.
patterns.why: Eye-tracking punishes clutter; career-center guides treat objective blocks as wasted top-third real estate for experienced-project candidates.
patterns.exemplar: The top third goes to headline + the #1 project, never to 'seeking a challenging opportunity…'.

patterns.rule: Prefer specific engineering verbs over generic ones: Built/Shipped/Engineered/Cut/Automated/Benchmarked/Instrumented > Developed/Created > Worked on/Helped (banned).
patterns.why: Verb specificity is the fastest craft lever in a 7-second skim; Harvard's verb guidance exists precisely because generic verbs blur real work.
patterns.exemplar: 'Worked on a resume tool' → 'Shipped a resume compiler with 100% parse-back fidelity'.

patterns.rule: Every project needs a jargon-free identity line before any mechanism bullet: what it is, for whom, in nouns a stranger recognizes. Internal vocabulary (module names, invented terms) means the reviewer skims past without comprehension.
patterns.why: The context line is what makes the following bullets legible as parts of a real system rather than disconnected mechanisms.
patterns.exemplar: A [familiar category noun] that [does plain-language Y] for [audience Z] — then and only then the how

patterns.rule: Name specific model versions and evaluation tooling where he actually used them — never stop at a bare "LLMs". Pair vector stores with their embedding models and tie orchestration frameworks to shipped features, never to a tool list.
patterns.why: In an 8,600-resume analysis, top scorers named model versions 3.4x more often, and naming an eval tool put candidates in the top quartile 2.6x as often — most resumes mention no evaluation at all, which reads as shipping unevaluated features.
patterns.exemplar: Built X on [specific model/version he used] with [the eval/guardrail he built], measured by [the real check]

patterns.rule: Frame LLM/agent work as deployed capability with its operational consequence — retrieval, tool use, guardrails, latency/cost handling serving real use — never as an experiment or demo.
patterns.why: Hiring teams for LLM/agent roles screen first for evidence of features shipped to real users; notebook demos are discounted on sight.
patterns.exemplar: Shipped [LLM capability] serving [real users/context], handling [operational hard part: latency, cost, guardrails]

patterns.rule: When no clean number exists, lead with the concrete problem, name what was built, and close with the ripple effect — what it unblocked, what class of failure it removed, whose work got easier. Specificity substitutes for quantity.
patterns.why: A fabricated percentage falls apart in an interview; a precise causal story does not.
patterns.exemplar: Rewrote X that was failing under condition Y, eliminating failure-class Z and unblocking W

patterns.rule: Where no measurement exists, state scope, frequency, a before/after state, or an eliminated failure mode — never an invented metric. Vague claims ("improved performance") are the failure, not the absence of digits.
patterns.why: Recruiters accept state-change framing as a legitimate measure; a made-up number is the #1 fraud they hunt.
patterns.exemplar: Reduced X from [old state] to [new state] by doing Z — a state change, not an invented percentage

patterns.rule: Vary bullet length and sentence construction deliberately across a block — never three bullets sharing one grammatical skeleton. Anchor each to a specific system, a specific failure, a specific choice only this candidate could have written.
patterns.why: Recruiters flag identical sentence rhythm and polish-without-particulars as AI-generated and discount the whole page.
patterns.exemplar: One specific system + one specific hard part per bullet, in varied shapes — rotate result-first / method-first / initiative-first

patterns.rule: The credible AI-work dimensions are operational: latency, token/inference cost, eval pass rates, hallucination-class reduction, retrieval precision. Name the dimension only where the work truly touched it — the dimension without a fake number still carries the signal.
patterns.why: These separate engineers who ran systems in production from those who called an API once.
patterns.exemplar: Reduced [operational dimension] by doing Z, verified by [the eval mechanism he built]

patterns.rule: Pair every project with whatever honest external proof exists — a live URL, user count, stars, a placement — stated in the text itself, not only as a link.
patterns.why: A description alone blurs into the pile; a validation signal makes it real, and reviewers may not click.
patterns.exemplar: Built X [description] — live at URL / used by N — the proof rides in the same line

patterns.rule: Render each project as: title with stack, one plain context line stating what it does and why it exists, then 2-3 bullets covering what was built, the hard engineering part, and an outcome or credibility signal. Engineering work, not a product pitch.
patterns.why: This is the block shape reviewers can actually parse in a skim; anything denser is skipped.
patterns.exemplar: TITLE | stack, then a plain "A [noun] that does X for Y." line, then: built A with hard-part B; engineered C producing D

patterns.rule: Use the exact term the job description uses wherever it accurately describes real evidence-backed experience, placed once in context — never as density stuffing, and never a near-synonym where the exact term is true.
patterns.why: ATS filtering is literal (near-synonyms miss); modern parsers score stuffing down. Coverage over repetition.
patterns.exemplar: [Action verb] + [the exact JD term, evidence-backed] + [result], once, in context

leadSignals: shipped LLM/agent product with live URL

pathBriefs.label: AI-first startup (his stated lane)
pathBriefs.summary: Fast, founder-adjacent hiring; portfolio and shipped artifacts outweigh DSA. Interviews vary widely; a live product with evals is the strongest signal. Interns contribute to production from week one.
pathBriefs.timeline: Rolling, short cycles (days-weeks); apply when a role opens, not by season.
pathBriefs.referralWeight: High — referred candidates interview at roughly 40-60% vs 2-3% for cold applications; a warm intro to a founder/eng lead is the highest-leverage single act.
pathBriefs.portfolioVsDsa: Portfolio-first. Expect a practical build/take-home or deep walkthrough of your own systems; DSA rounds are lighter or absent.
pathBriefs.conversionNorms: Less formalized than big tech; strong interns often convert to part-time/full-time offers quickly because teams are small.
pathBriefs.indiaVsRemote: Remote-international is realistic if async-writing signals are visible; state IST overlap honestly.

pathBriefs.label: Research lab / AI residency
pathBriefs.summary: Screens for research rigor: strong math foundations, reproducible projects, open-source contributions or publications. Residencies exist precisely for non-traditional entrants with proof of ability.
pathBriefs.timeline: Annual cohorts with hard deadlines (typically fall-winter applications for the following year); missing the window costs a full year.
pathBriefs.referralWeight: Moderate — a researcher's endorsement helps, but artifacts (papers, repos, rigorous evals) carry the decision.
pathBriefs.portfolioVsDsa: Artifacts + fundamentals. Expect research discussion and ML-depth interviews over LeetCode grinding.
pathBriefs.conversionNorms: Residencies frequently convert to research engineer/scientist roles; treat the residency itself as the pipeline.
pathBriefs.indiaVsRemote: Most residencies are on-site (US/UK) with visa support varying; some labs run remote-friendly internships — check per-program.

pathBriefs.label: Big-tech internship pipeline
pathBriefs.summary: Structured, season-locked, DSA-gated. The primary pipeline to full-time at these companies (~70-80% of their engineers interned first), but it is also the lane most saturated with cold applicants.
pathBriefs.timeline: Applications open ~8-12 months before the summer (Aug-Oct for the following year); on-campus/off-campus drives in India follow their own calendars.
pathBriefs.referralWeight: High for getting the screen — referrals are a large share of interviews — but the DSA bar is unbendable regardless of referral.
pathBriefs.portfolioVsDsa: DSA-first. At least two dedicated coding rounds; projects differentiate at the margin but cannot substitute for fundamentals.
pathBriefs.conversionNorms: Intern→full-time conversion averages ~63% industry-wide (2024-25 class, five-year high); top programs convert at 70-80%+.
pathBriefs.indiaVsRemote: India offices (Bengaluru/Hyderabad/Gurgaon) run their own intern drives; international remote internships at these firms are rare.
